narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the effort you've put into your undergraduate studies, LSAT preparation, and building your future. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 40.5% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. This decision was driven primarily by a few key aspects of your profile compared to typical applicants. Your LSAT score of 34 was a strong point—it ranked solidly among applicants and pushed the model toward predicting you'd pass, better than it might have otherwise. However, your Year 3 school decile of 2, which is notably lower than the average of around 5.5, had the strongest pull in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar failure and outweighing your other strengths. Your Year 1 decile of 3 and middle family income bracket both offered some positive leanings toward success, similar to many who pass, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 was a bit below the typical 3.2 but didn't sway things much. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority of successful bar takers, so it helped slightly too. Overall, your profile stands out as having a dip in later academic decile performance relative to peers who typically pass, even with your commendable LSAT and typical enrollment status. While this is disappointing, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive academic paths, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted improvement in later years, perhaps retaking the LSAT for an even higher score, or gaining experiences that highlight your resilience. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas or explore programs with holistic reviews that value your background. Your potential is clear—keep pushing forward.
